1:
On Colossians 3:1 — “If ye then be risen with Christ, seek those things which are above, where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God” — the witnesses mark the call to heavenly-mindedness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the condition. Robertson: “if then ye were raised with Christ” (ei…synegerthete), assumed true, the other half of baptism’s picture (2:12). Ellicott: the resurrection with Christ (Romans 6:1–14). The Puritan Henry: inferred from our privileges — duty follows. Wesley: “as Christ being risen, immediately went to heaven.”

The verse turns from the negative to the positive. Since you are risen with Christ, seek the things above, where Christ reigns at God’s right hand. The new life looks upward.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from refuting error to the positive pursuit of the risen life. The witnesses note the assumed fact: “ye are risen with Christ” — the other side of the death with Christ; in union with him, believers share his resurrection, raised to new life. They mark the inference drawn: “seek those things which are above” — the duty flows from the privilege; the risen believer is to direct his desires, aims, and pursuits heavenward. They observe the ground and motive: “where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God” — the believer’s life and treasure are where his risen Lord reigns, exalted in glory; as Christ ascended, so the heart that is one with him follows him up. They note Henry’s point that the practical duty is inferred from the doctrinal privilege — freedom from the law’s yoke does not free us from holy living but calls us to a higher pursuit. They observe that against the petty, earthbound rules of the false teachers, Paul sets a far nobler aim: not “touch not, taste not,” but “seek the things above.” The verse thus opens the practical heart of the epistle: the risen believer’s true exercise is not ascetic regulation of earthly things but the heavenward seeking of Christ — a mind set on things above, as the next verse presses.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from refuting error to the positive pursuit of the risen life. The assumed fact: “ye are risen with Christ” — the other side of the death with Christ; in union with him, believers share his resurrection. The inference: “seek those things which are above” — the duty flows from the privilege; the risen believer directs his desires and pursuits heavenward. The ground: “where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God” — the believer’s treasure is where his risen Lord reigns. Against the petty, earthbound rules of the false teachers, Paul sets a nobler aim: not “touch not, taste not,” but “seek the things above.”
2:
On Colossians 3:2 — “Set your affection on things above, not on things on the earth” — the witnesses mark the direction of the mind.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “set your mind on” (phroneite), “keep on thinking about” — not that we never think of earthly things, but these “should not absorb” us. Adam Clarke: “love heavenly things; study them.” The Reformed Calvin: not riches or houses, the things of the present life we must use (1 Corinthians 7:31).

The verse deepens the call. Set your mind on things above, not on things on the earth. The whole bent of thought and affection is to be heavenward.

So the witnesses see Paul press the heavenward seeking inward, to the very set of the mind. The witnesses note the word: “set your affection” or, better, set your mind — keep on thinking about, cherishing, and being occupied with the things above. They mark that this goes beyond outward seeking to the inward disposition: the believer’s habitual thoughts, loves, and interests are to be fixed on heaven and on Christ, not absorbed by earthly things. They observe the careful balance the expositors draw: this does not forbid all thought of earthly matters, which must be attended to, nor does it condemn the lawful use of the present life; rather, as Calvin notes, it forbids letting these things engross and dominate the heart. They note the contrast: “not on things on the earth” — the worldly, the transient, the merely material, including the very ceremonial trifles the false teachers magnified. They observe that the cure for earthbound religion and earthbound living alike is a mind set above. The verse thus completes the inward turn: not only are the risen believer’s pursuits to be heavenward (v. 1), but his very thinking and affection are to dwell there — for the reasons the next verses give: he has died, his life is hid with Christ, and Christ is his life and coming glory.

So the witnesses see Paul press the heavenward seeking inward, to the very set of the mind. “Set your affection” or, better, set your mind — keep on thinking about, cherishing, and being occupied with the things above. This goes beyond outward seeking to the inward disposition: the believer’s habitual thoughts, loves, and interests fixed on heaven and Christ. The balance the expositors draw: this does not forbid all thought of earthly matters or the lawful use of the present life, but, as Calvin notes, forbids letting these engross the heart. The cure for earthbound religion and earthbound living alike is a mind set above.
3:
On Colossians 3:3 — “For ye are dead, and your life is hid with Christ in God” — the witnesses mark the hidden life of the believer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “ye died” (apethanete, to sin, Romans 6:2); “is hid” (kekryptai, perfect, remains concealed) locked “with Christ in God” — “no hellish burglar can break that combination.” Adam Clarke: “Christ is your treasure.” Wesley: the life “laid up in God, with Christ.”

The verse gives the reason for heavenly-mindedness. You died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. The believer’s true life is safely concealed above, in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the heavenward mind in the believer’s death and hidden life. The witnesses note the first reason: “ye are dead” — you died, in union with Christ, to sin and to the world; the old life is ended. They mark the second, precious truth: “your life is hid with Christ in God” — the believer’s real, spiritual life is not visible to the world, nor even fully manifest to himself, but is “hidden,” safely stored up, in Christ, who is himself in God. They observe the security the figure implies: the life is locked away with Christ in God, beyond the reach of every enemy — as Robertson vividly puts it, no thief can break that combination. They note that the hiddenness explains why the world does not recognize the believer (it sees only the outward man) and why the Christian must look heavenward for his treasure and life, since they are above with Christ. They observe Clarke’s point that Christ himself is the believer’s treasure, and where the treasure is, there the heart should be. They observe that this hiddenness is not loss but safekeeping, awaiting the manifestation of glory. The verse thus supplies the deep ground of the heavenly mind: the believer’s life is dead to the world and hidden with Christ in God — and therefore his thoughts belong where his life is, until that life is openly revealed at Christ’s appearing.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the heavenward mind in the believer’s death and hidden life. The first reason: “ye are dead” — you died, in union with Christ, to sin and the world. The second, precious truth: “your life is hid with Christ in God” — the believer’s real, spiritual life is not visible to the world but safely stored up in Christ, who is in God. The security the figure implies: the life is locked away with Christ in God, beyond every enemy’s reach — no thief can break that combination. The hiddenness explains why the world does not recognize the believer and why the Christian must look heavenward for his treasure and life.
4:
On Colossians 3:4 — “When Christ, who is our life, shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory” — the witnesses mark the future manifestation in glory.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the hope. Robertson: “when Christ shall be manifested” (phanerothe), the second coming, certain but undated. Barnes: then Christians “shall be raised… and ascend with the Redeemer” (1 Thessalonians 4:16). Ellicott: the last stage — “glorification with Christ in heaven” (1 John 3:2). The Reformed Calvin: a choice consolation.

The verse crowns the hope. When Christ our life appears, the believer too will appear with him in glory. The now-hidden life will be openly revealed.

So the witnesses see Paul lift the hidden life to its glorious future manifestation. The witnesses note the great title: “Christ, who is our life” — not merely the giver of life but our life itself, the fountain and substance of the believer’s being; our life is hidden because it is bound up in him. They mark the certain but undated event: “when Christ… shall appear” — his second coming, longed for though its time is unknown, when he will be openly manifested in glory. They observe the believer’s share: “then shall ye also appear with him in glory” — the life now hidden will then be revealed; the saints, raised and transformed, will be displayed in the very glory of their Lord, sharing his manifestation. They note Calvin’s point that this is a choice consolation: the present hiddenness and lowliness of the believer is not the end; the day is coming when all will be made glorious. They observe the comfort against the world’s contempt and the believer’s own present obscurity: what is hidden now will be gloriously shown then. The verse thus completes the foundation of the heavenly mind: the believer’s life, now dead to the world and hidden with Christ, will be manifested in glory at his appearing — a hope that draws the heart upward and grounds the call, in the verses following, to mortify the earthly and put on the heavenly.
5:
On Colossians 3:5 — “Mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth; fornication, uncleanness, inordinate affection, evil concupiscence, and covetousness, which is idolatry” — the witnesses mark the call to put sin to death.

The Eastern fathers note the demand. Chrysostom: “Mortify, he saith, your members.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “mortify” (nekrosate, put to death, treat as dead), applying the death metaphor (2:20; 3:3) to actual life. Adam Clarke: “use no member of your body to sin.” Wesley: the members “which together make up the body of sin… covetousness… idolatry.”

The verse turns the doctrine into duty. Put to death your earthly members — fornication, impurity, lust, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry. The new life requires the slaying of sin.

So the witnesses see Paul draw the practical inference: because you have died and risen with Christ, put sin to death. The witnesses note the force of “mortify” — put to death, slay, treat as dead with a continued stroke; the death already true in principle (vv. 3–4) is to be made actual in the daily slaying of sin. They mark the figure: “your members which are upon the earth” — not the bodily limbs themselves, but the sinful passions that work through them, the earthly “body of sin.” They observe the catalogue of vices to be slain, mounting from act to root: “fornication, uncleanness” — sins of impurity in deed; “inordinate affection, evil concupiscence” — the disordered passions and lusts within; and “covetousness, which is idolatry” — the greedy craving that sets the heart on gain as its god. They note the striking equation: covetousness is idolatry, for it gives to created things the love and trust due to God alone. They observe that this is the true mortification, against the false asceticism of chapter 2: not the neglecting of the body in will-worship, but the putting to death of the soul’s sins. The verse thus opens the new man’s ethic with the slaying of the old: the risen believer is to mortify the earthly passions — impurity, lust, and greed — for these belong to the dead life he has left and call down God’s wrath.
6:
On Colossians 3:6 — “For which things’ sake the wrath of God cometh on the children of disobedience” — the witnesses mark the motive of holy fear.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the warning. Robertson: “cometh the wrath of God” — Paul does not regard these sins as indifferent (Ephesians 5:6). Adam Clarke: “God is angry… and inflicts the punishment they deserve.” The Reformed Calvin notes the present tense may stand for the future, accommodated to either meaning. Wesley: “though the heathens lightly regarded them.”

The verse gives a reason for mortifying sin: the wrath of God. For these very sins God’s wrath falls on the disobedient. Their gravity is shown by the judgment they incur.

So the witnesses see Paul press the slaying of sin by the warning of divine wrath. The witnesses note that the sins just named — impurity, lust, greed — are no light matters, however lightly the heathen regarded them; “for which things’ sake the wrath of God cometh.” They mark that this answers any notion that such fleshly sins are trivial: God’s holy anger is provoked by them and falls upon “the children of disobedience” — those characterized by rebellion against God, who give themselves to these vices. They observe Calvin’s note that the present tense (“cometh”) may point to the wrath already at work and to the wrath to come, both true: God’s judgment pursues these sins now and in the day of reckoning. They note the contrast implied: the believer, no longer a child of disobedience, must therefore have done with the sins that bring such wrath. They observe that the fear of God’s wrath is a legitimate and weighty motive to mortification, set alongside the higher motives of union with the risen Christ. The verse thus reinforces the call of verse 5: these earthly sins are to be put to death because they are precisely what provokes the wrath of God upon the disobedient — sins the believer once lived in but must now utterly forsake, as the next verse reminds.

So the witnesses see Paul press the slaying of sin by the warning of divine wrath. The sins just named — impurity, lust, greed — are no light matters, however lightly the heathen regarded them; “for which things’ sake the wrath of God cometh.” God’s holy anger is provoked by them and falls on “the children of disobedience.” Calvin’s note: the present tense may point to wrath already at work and to wrath to come, both true. The believer, no longer a child of disobedience, must have done with the sins that bring such wrath; the fear of God’s wrath is a legitimate motive to mortification.
7:
On Colossians 3:7 — “In the which ye also walked some time, when ye lived in them” — the witnesses mark the believers’ former life in these sins.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the past. Robertson: “walked aforetime” (periepatesate pote, constative aorist) in the pagan state; “when ye lived” (ezete, imperfect, used to live), a sharp distinction of tenses. Ellicott: their “active participation” — “ye walked in them.” Wesley: “living denotes the inward principle; walking, the outward acts.”

The verse recalls their past. In these sins they too once walked, when they lived in them. The reminder shames them out of returning to what they have left.

So the witnesses see Paul remind the Colossians that these sins were once their own way of life. The witnesses note the candid recollection: “in the which ye also walked” — they were no strangers to these vices but had themselves practiced them in their heathen past. They mark the precise distinction of tenses the expositors draw: “ye walked” (the settled habit of conduct, the outward acts) “when ye lived in them” (the inner life and element in which they then existed) — Wesley’s point that living denotes the inward principle, walking the outward acts. They observe that the sins named were the very atmosphere of their former, unconverted state, surrounding them with contagion on every side. They note the force of the reminder: it both humbles them (they were once such) and urges them on (they have left that life). They observe that grace had made the decisive break — “some time,” once, but no longer. The verse thus heightens the call to mortify sin by recalling its origin: these are the deeds of the old life the believers themselves once lived; to return to them would be to go back to the death from which they were raised. It sets up the emphatic “but now” of the next verse, which marks the great change.

So the witnesses see Paul remind the Colossians that these sins were once their own way of life. The candid recollection: “in the which ye also walked” — they were no strangers to these vices but had practiced them in their heathen past. The precise distinction of tenses: “ye walked” (the settled habit, the outward acts) “when ye lived in them” (the inner life and element) — Wesley’s point that living denotes the inward principle, walking the outward acts. The sins were the very atmosphere of their former state. The reminder both humbles them and urges them on: grace had made the decisive break — once, but no longer.
8:
On Colossians 3:8 — “But now ye also put off all these; anger, wrath, malice, blasphemy, filthy communication out of your mouth” — the witnesses mark the second catalogue, of the sins of temper and tongue.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the new list. Robertson: “but now” (nyni de), emphatic contrast to “some time” (v. 7); “put away” (apothesthe), lay aside like old clothes. The Puritan Henry: as we mortify inordinate appetites, so inordinate passions. Ellicott: “blasphemy” here is slander; the filthy word is “foul” abuse.

The verse turns to the sins of temper and speech. But now put away all these: anger, wrath, malice, slander, and foul speech. The new life lays aside these as worn-out garments.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the grosser sins of the flesh to the sins of temper and tongue, equally to be cast off. The witnesses note the emphatic “but now” — the sharp contrast with the “some time” of the old life; the resurrection life of chapters 2–3 marks a decisive change. They mark the figure: “put off” — lay aside, strip off like filthy, outworn clothing, the same metaphor of the old garments. They observe the catalogue, a second list answering the first: “anger” (the settled feeling), “wrath” (the heated outburst), “malice” (the ill-will that wishes harm), “blasphemy” (here slander and railing, evil-speaking against God or men), and “filthy communication” (foul, abusive, shameful speech). They note Henry’s point that these spiritual sins of passion, though less gross than the fleshly vices, are no less contrary to the gospel and must be mortified too. They observe Wesley’s wonder that even saints needed warning against such things — a reminder of how much remains to be put off. The verse thus extends the mortification to the inner life of temper and the outer life of speech: the believer who has put off the old man must strip away anger, malice, and foul words as belonging to the cast-off garment of the former life.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the grosser sins of the flesh to the sins of temper and tongue. The emphatic “but now” — the sharp contrast with the old life; the resurrection life marks a decisive change. The figure: “put off” — strip off like filthy, outworn clothing. The catalogue: “anger” (the settled feeling), “wrath” (the heated outburst), “malice” (the ill-will), “blasphemy” (here slander and railing), and “filthy communication” (foul, abusive speech). Henry’s point: these spiritual sins of passion, though less gross than the fleshly vices, are no less contrary to the gospel and must be mortified too.
9:
On Colossians 3:9 — “Lie not one to another, seeing that ye have put off the old man with his deeds” — the witnesses mark the prohibition of lying.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ground. Robertson: “lie not” (me pseudesthe), stop lying or do not have the habit; put more pointedly. Ellicott compares Ephesians 4:25, noting the absent clause “members one of another.” The Reformed Calvin: he condemns all cunning and base deception, contrasted with sincerity.

The verse forbids falsehood on the ground of the changed nature. Do not lie to one another, since you have put off the old self with its practices. Truth befits the new man.

So the witnesses see Paul forbid lying as inconsistent with the put-off old man. The witnesses note the command: “lie not one to another” — whether to cease the habit of lying or never to begin it; falsehood, which might have been included in the previous list, is singled out for its destructiveness to fellowship. They mark Calvin’s breadth: this condemns not merely outright falsehood but all cunning, base artifice, and deception, set in contrast to sincerity. They observe the ground given: “seeing that ye have put off the old man with his deeds” — the old corrupt nature, with its whole train of evil practices, has been stripped away in conversion; lying belongs to that discarded self and is utterly unfitting for those who have put it off. They note the comparison the expositors draw with Ephesians 4:25, where the reason given is “we are members one of another”; here the reason is the change of nature — the old man put off, the new put on. They observe that the new life and the old vice cannot consist together: to lie is to wear again the garment one has cast away. The verse thus grounds truthfulness in the believer’s transformed identity: having put off the old man, he must put away the old man’s lying — and, as the next verse declares, having put on the new man, he is being renewed in the image of his Creator.
10:
On Colossians 3:10 — “And have put on the new man, which is renewed in knowledge after the image of him that created him” — the witnesses mark the renewal in God’s image.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the renewal. Robertson: “have put on” (endysamenoi) the new man (neon, young); cf. Galatians 3:27. Barnes: to the renewed soul knowledge is imparted, as man was at first created. Adam Clarke: ignorance the mark of heathenism, knowledge of the Christian. The Reformed Calvin: newness consists in the illumination of the Spirit, lively and effectual.

The verse gives the positive side: the new man put on. The believer has put on the new self, being renewed in knowledge after the image of its Creator. Conversion restores the divine image, beginning with knowledge.

So the witnesses see Paul set the putting on of the new man beside the putting off of the old. The witnesses note the matching figure: as the old man was stripped off (v. 9), so the “new man” — the new, fresh nature created in regeneration — is “put on,” worn as a garment. They mark its ongoing transformation: “renewed in knowledge” — the new nature is continually being made new, and that especially in true knowledge of God, which (Calvin notes) is no bare intellectual light but the lively, effectual illumination of the Spirit, transforming the whole man. They observe the pattern of the renewal: “after the image of him that created him” — the new man is being remade into the likeness of God, who created it, the image of God lost in Adam being restored in Christ. They note Clarke’s point that ignorance was the mark of the heathen state, true knowledge of the Christian; the renewed soul recovers, in knowledge, what man had at his first creation. They observe that this renewal in the divine image is the ground of all the graces to follow: because the new man bears God’s likeness, it is to display God’s character. The verse thus completes the great change: the believer, having put off the old self, has put on a new self that is being renewed in knowledge into the very image of its Creator — a renewal that abolishes, as the next verse shows, all the old divisions of men.
11:
On Colossians 3:11 — “Where there is neither Greek nor Jew, circumcision nor uncircumcision, Barbarian, Scythian, bond nor free: but Christ is all, and in all” — the witnesses mark the abolition of human distinctions.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the leveling. Robertson: “there cannot be” (ouk eni, does not exist); race distinctions vanish in the new man (cf. Galatians 3:28). Adam Clarke: no inquiry made what nation; religion, culture, status of no account. Wesley: Scythian, “of all barbarians most barbarous”; “Christ is in all that are thus renewed, and is all things in them.”

The verse declares the new humanity’s unity. In it there is no distinction of nation, religion, culture, or class: Christ is all and in all. The divisions of the old world dissolve in him.

So the witnesses see Paul proclaim that in the new man every human division is abolished. The witnesses note the sweep of the distinctions named and dissolved: “neither Greek nor Jew” — national difference; “circumcision nor uncircumcision” — religious and ceremonial difference, the very thing the false teachers pressed; “Barbarian, Scythian” — cultural difference, the Scythian being the byword for the rudest savage; and “bond nor free” — social difference, slave and master. They mark that in the new humanity created in Christ, none of these counts: all are on a level, one in him. They observe the glorious reason: “but Christ is all, and in all” — Christ is everything, the sum and substance of the new man, and he dwells in all the renewed alike, whatever their earthly background. They note Wesley’s point that what a man is externally matters nothing; Christ is in all who are renewed and is all things in them. They observe how this strikes at the Colossian error, which made circumcision and ceremonial status matter; in Christ such distinctions are nothing. The verse thus crowns the doctrine of the new man with its unifying truth: the renewed bear one image, God’s, and own one all-sufficient Christ, so that every division of race, religion, culture, and class is swallowed up — the ground on which the graces of love and forbearance that follow are built.
12:
On Colossians 3:12 — “Put on therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved, bowels of mercies, kindness, humbleness of mind, meekness, longsuffering” — the witnesses mark the graces of the new man.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the clothing figure. Robertson: “put on” (endysasthe), the new garment; “as God’s elect” (cf. Romans 8:33). Ellicott: elect, holy, and beloved — applied to the whole church. The Puritan Henry: not only put off anger but put on compassion. The Reformed Calvin: these graces are “the effects and evidences of renovation.”

The verse turns to the positive graces to be worn. As God’s chosen, holy, and beloved, put on compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience. The new man is clothed with Christlike virtues.

So the witnesses see Paul clothe the new man with the gentle graces, as the old was stripped of its vices. The witnesses note the dignifying address: “as the elect of God, holy and beloved” — the believers are chosen by God, set apart as holy, and dearly loved; this high standing is the motive and pattern for the graces they are to wear. They mark the figure carried on: “put on” — clothe yourselves, as with a new garment, with the virtues now named. They observe the catalogue of tender graces: “bowels of mercies” — heartfelt compassion; “kindness” — gentle goodness toward others; “humbleness of mind” — lowliness, the opposite of the false “humility” of chapter 2; “meekness” — the gentle, unirritable spirit; and “longsuffering” — patient endurance of others’ faults. They note Henry’s point that the new man not only ceases to do evil but learns to do well, putting on compassion as well as putting off anger. They observe Calvin’s that these graces are the very evidences of renovation, proving the new nature real. The verse thus opens the new man’s wardrobe of virtues: the chosen, holy, beloved people of God are to clothe themselves with the merciful, kind, humble, meek, and patient temper that befits their calling — graces that issue, in the next verse, in mutual forbearance and forgiveness.

So the witnesses see Paul clothe the new man with the gentle graces, as the old was stripped of its vices. The dignifying address: “as the elect of God, holy and beloved” — chosen, set apart, dearly loved; this high standing the motive and pattern. The figure carried on: “put on” — clothe yourselves with the virtues now named: “bowels of mercies” (heartfelt compassion), “kindness” (gentle goodness), “humbleness of mind” (lowliness, the opposite of the false humility of chapter 2), “meekness,” and “longsuffering.” Calvin’s point: these graces are the very evidences of renovation, proving the new nature real.
13:
On Colossians 3:13 — “Forbearing one another, and forgiving one another, if any man have a quarrel against any: even as Christ forgave you, so also do ye” — the witnesses mark mutual forbearance and forgiveness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the pattern. Robertson: “forbearing” (anechomenoi, holding yourselves back) and “forgiving” (charizomenoi, freely). Ellicott: the better reading is “the Lord forgave you.” Adam Clarke: forgive on the first acknowledgment, “even as Christ… required no satisfaction.” Wesley: forbearing “if anything is now wrong,” forgiving “what is past.”

The verse adds the social graces of patience and pardon. Bear with one another and forgive any grievance, as Christ forgave you. The forgiven are to forgive.

So the witnesses see the gentle graces issue in mutual forbearance and forgiveness. The witnesses note the two duties: “forbearing one another” — holding back, bearing patiently with one another’s faults, weaknesses, and provocations, not retaliating; and “forgiving one another” — freely pardoning any grievance or complaint that one may have against another. They mark Wesley’s neat distinction: forbearance for present faults, forgiveness for past ones. They observe the great pattern and motive: “even as Christ forgave you” — the believer’s forgiveness is measured by, and flows from, Christ’s own free, full pardon; as he forgave freely, requiring no satisfaction from us and seeking only our good, so are we to forgive. They note Clarke’s point that we are to forgive readily, on the first acknowledgment of the fault, after the model of Christ. They observe that this lifts forgiveness above mere natural good nature to a Christlike grace grounded in the gospel: the freely forgiven cannot withhold forgiveness from others. The verse thus completes the social graces of the new man: clothed with compassion and meekness, the believer is to bear with and freely forgive his brethren, taking the forgiving Christ as his rule — and over all these graces, as the next verse declares, to put on love.

So the witnesses see the gentle graces issue in mutual forbearance and forgiveness. The two duties: “forbearing one another” — bearing patiently with one another’s faults, not retaliating; and “forgiving one another” — freely pardoning any grievance. Wesley’s distinction: forbearance for present faults, forgiveness for past ones. The great pattern: “even as Christ forgave you” — the believer’s forgiveness measured by, and flowing from, Christ’s own free, full pardon. This lifts forgiveness above mere natural good nature to a Christlike grace grounded in the gospel: the freely forgiven cannot withhold forgiveness from others.
14:
On Colossians 3:14 — “And above all these things put on charity, which is the bond of perfectness” — the witnesses mark love as the crowning grace.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the supremacy. Robertson: “put on love” (ten agapen), supplied from v. 12; “the bond of perfectness.” Adam Clarke: love is the outer garment that envelops all the rest, “over all.” Ellicott: “the general principle, harmonising all the special graces.” Wesley: love “contains the whole of Christian perfection.”

The verse crowns the graces with love. Above all, put on love, which binds all the rest into perfect unity. Love is the chief and uniting grace.

So the witnesses see Paul set love over all the other graces as their crown and bond. The witnesses note the supremacy: “above all these things” — over and upon all the virtues just named, as the outer garment is put on over the rest, love is to be worn. They mark the figure Clarke draws: as the outer robe envelops and holds together all the clothing beneath, so love envelops and unites all the other graces; even compassion and kindness derive their feeling and power from love. They observe the great description: “the bond of perfectness” — love is the ligament that binds all the virtues together into one harmonious, complete whole; without it the graces would fall apart, but love unites them into the perfection of Christian character. They note Wesley’s and Ellicott’s point that love contains the whole of Christian perfection and harmonizes all the special graces, connecting their parts. They observe that this echoes the great chapter on love: love is the more excellent way, the sum and crown of all. The verse thus places love at the summit of the new man’s graces: it is not one virtue among many but the bond that holds all together and brings them to perfection — the crowning garment over compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, patience, and forgiveness. So clothed, the believer is fitted for the rule of Christ’s peace within, which the next verse enjoins.

So the witnesses see Paul set love over all the other graces as their crown and bond. “Above all these things” — over and upon all the virtues, as the outer garment is put on over the rest, love is to be worn. Clarke’s figure: as the outer robe envelops and holds together all the clothing beneath, so love envelops and unites all the graces. The great description: “the bond of perfectness” — love is the ligament binding all the virtues into one harmonious, complete whole. Wesley’s point: love contains the whole of Christian perfection — not one virtue among many but the bond that brings all to perfection.
15:
On Colossians 3:15 — “And let the peace of God rule in your hearts, to the which also ye are called in one body; and be ye thankful” — the witnesses mark the rule of Christ’s peace.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the umpire figure. Robertson: “rule” (brabeueto), to act as umpire (brabeus); “the peace of Christ” (the true reading), which Christ gives (John 14:27). Adam Clarke notes the strong manuscript support for “Christ.” Ellicott: the link between love of man and thankfulness to God (Philippians 4:7). The Reformed Calvin: let it “judge, decide, and govern.”

The verse enjoins the inward rule of peace. Let Christ’s peace act as umpire in your hearts, the peace to which you were called in one body; and be thankful. Peace and gratitude govern the new life.

So the witnesses see Paul set the peace of Christ to govern the heart and gratitude to fill it. The witnesses note the striking figure in “rule” — to act as the umpire or judge at the games, who decides the contest and awards the prize; so Christ’s peace is to preside in the heart, deciding and settling every inward conflict, governing the affections and quieting strife. They mark the better reading, “the peace of Christ” — the peace that Christ gives and is, ruling where love (v. 14) reigns. They observe the corporate calling: “to the which also ye are called in one body” — believers are called to this peace as members of one body, so that the peace which rules each heart also binds the church together; discord among members violates the calling. They note the closing exhortation: “be ye thankful” — gratitude is to accompany peace, the thankful heart being a peaceful one. They observe Ellicott’s point that this verse links the love of man just urged with the thankfulness to God now enjoined. The verse thus turns from the graces toward others to the inward government of the heart: Christ’s peace is to umpire within, settling strife and ruling the affections, in keeping with the one-body calling, and crowned with thankfulness — an inward order that the indwelling word of Christ, in the next verse, will deepen.
16:
On Colossians 3:16 — “Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom; teaching and admonishing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing with grace in your hearts to the Lord” — the witnesses mark the indwelling word and worship.

The Eastern fathers note the means of thankfulness. Chrysostom links it to thanksgiving. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “the word of Christ” (only here), subjective or objective genitive; psalms, hymns, spiritual songs. Adam Clarke: “be well instructed in the doctrine of Christ.” Wesley: let it “take up its stated residence… in the largest measure.”

The verse enjoins the rich indwelling of Christ’s word and mutual song. Let Christ’s word dwell in you abundantly, teaching one another and singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs with grace to the Lord. The word fills the heart and overflows in worship.

So the witnesses see Paul enjoin the rich indwelling of Christ’s word, issuing in mutual instruction and song. The witnesses note the command: “let the word of Christ dwell in you” — not pay a passing visit but take up settled residence; and “richly” — in the fullest measure, abundantly, filling and governing the heart; “in all wisdom” — producing the spirit of true wisdom. They mark that this indwelling word is the source of the church’s mutual ministry: “teaching and admonishing one another” — the believers, full of the word, instructing and warning each other in love. They observe the means and overflow: “in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs” — sacred song as a vehicle of teaching and worship; “singing with grace in your hearts to the Lord” — the praise springing from grace within and directed to the Lord, heart-worship, not mere sound. They note Wesley’s point that the word is to dwell in the largest measure and with the greatest efficacy. They observe that against the false teachers’ esoteric speculations, Paul sets the plain word of Christ richly known and sung by all. The verse thus enriches the inward life of the new man: the word of Christ dwelling abundantly, overflowing in mutual teaching and grace-filled song to the Lord — the soil from which the all-embracing rule of the next verse springs.
17:
On Colossians 3:17 — “And whatsoever ye do in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God and the Father by him” — the witnesses mark the all-embracing rule of life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the comprehensiveness. Robertson: “whatsoever ye do” (pan ho ti ean poiete), everything whatever; “do all” in the name of the Lord Jesus. Ellicott compares 1 Corinthians 10:31 — “do all to the glory of God”; this the “first principle of all godly life.” Adam Clarke: begin and end with him, invoking his name.

The verse gives the governing principle of all conduct. Whatever you do, in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, with thanksgiving to God. The whole life is to be done for Christ and with gratitude.

So the witnesses see Paul gather the whole of life under one all-embracing rule. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “whatsoever ye do in word or deed” — everything, without exception, whether spoken or done, in worship or daily affairs, falls under this command. They mark the rule itself: “do all in the name of the Lord Jesus” — by his authority, in dependence on him, for his glory, and as those who represent him; every word and act is to be such as can be done in his name and offered to him. They observe Ellicott’s point that this is the first principle of all godly life, parallel to “do all to the glory of God” — the great aim that consecrates the whole of conduct, lifting even the commonest action into the service of Christ. They note the accompanying spirit: “giving thanks to God and the Father by him” — all to be done with gratitude, through Christ the Mediator, so that thanksgiving permeates the whole life. They observe that this single principle sums up Christian ethics: not a multitude of petty rules like the false teachers’ “touch not, taste not,” but the one comprehensive aim of doing all for Christ. The verse thus crowns the new man’s walk with its governing maxim: every word and deed in the name of the Lord Jesus, with thanksgiving to God — the principle that then orders the particular relationships of the household, which the closing verses address.
18:
On Colossians 3:18 — “Wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands, as it is fit in the Lord” — the witnesses mark the first of the household duties.

The Eastern fathers note the placement. Chrysostom: he gives these commands here as in Ephesians and the Pastorals, perhaps for dissensions. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “be in subjection” (hypotassesthe); “own” not in the text here. Wesley: a military term, “entire submission… as soldiers to a general” (Ephesians 5:22).

The verse opens the household code. Wives are to submit to their husbands, as is fitting in the Lord. The new life orders the home.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the general rule of life to the particular duties of the household, beginning with wives. The witnesses note that, having laid down that all is to be done in the Lord’s name, Paul now applies it to the relationships of the home — the briefer parallel to the fuller treatment in Ephesians. They mark the first duty: “wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands” — the willing subjection of the wife to her husband’s headship, an ordered yielding within the marriage relation. They observe the qualification that hallows and limits it: “as it is fit in the Lord” — the submission is what is becoming and proper in the Christian relationship, rendered as part of obedience to the Lord, and therefore within the bounds of his will, not extending to what is contrary to him. They note that this is no servile or degrading subjection but the fitting order of the home as God appointed, transfigured now by being “in the Lord.” They observe Chrysostom’s suggestion that Paul gives these household commands where they were needed, perhaps amid dissensions. The verse thus begins the Christian household code: the wife’s loving submission, offered as fitting in the Lord — the first of the paired duties that order the home, each member’s duty matched by the corresponding duty of the other, as the husband’s love in the next verse shows.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the general rule of life to the duties of the household, beginning with wives. Having laid down that all is to be done in the Lord’s name, he applies it to the home — the briefer parallel to Ephesians. The first duty: “wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands” — the willing subjection of the wife to her husband’s headship. The qualification that hallows it: “as it is fit in the Lord” — what is becoming in the Christian relationship, rendered as part of obedience to the Lord, within the bounds of his will. No servile subjection, but the fitting order of the home, transfigured by being “in the Lord.”
19:
On Colossians 3:19 — “Husbands, love your wives, and be not bitter against them” — the witnesses mark the husband’s duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the balance. Robertson: “love your wives” (present, “keep on loving”); “be not bitter” (me pikrainesthe), stop being bitter, the husband’s besetting sin. Ellicott: “grow not bitter, suffer not yourselves to be exasperated.” Adam Clarke: “where love is wanting… there is hell upon earth.” Wesley: bitterness “in word or spirit.”

The verse gives the matching duty. Husbands are to love their wives and not be harsh toward them. Authority is to be exercised in love, not bitterness.

So the witnesses see Paul match the wife’s submission with the husband’s love. The witnesses note the positive command: “husbands, love your wives” — a continual, abiding love, the present tense marking it as the steady habit of the heart; the husband’s headship is to be one of love, not domination. They mark the negative warning: “be not bitter against them” — stop being, or never become, harsh, sharp, resentful, or exasperated toward the wife; bitterness, the expositors note, is the husband’s besetting sin, the corruption of his authority. They observe Ellicott’s rendering — grow not bitter, suffer not yourselves to be embittered — and Wesley’s point that bitterness may be in word or in spirit, even without open anger. They note Clarke’s stark warning that where love is wanting in marriage, there is hell upon earth. They observe the balance of the household code: the wife’s submission is answered by the husband’s love, each duty guarding the other from abuse — submission without love would be tyranny, love without order would be weakness, but the two together make the Christian home. The verse thus completes the marriage pair: the husband is to love his wife steadily and to guard against the bitterness that poisons the home — an authority sweetened by love, as the parallel in Ephesians measures it by the love of Christ for the church.

So the witnesses see Paul match the wife’s submission with the husband’s love. The positive command: “love your wives” — a continual, abiding love, the present tense marking the steady habit of the heart; headship of love, not domination. The negative warning: “be not bitter against them” — the husband’s besetting sin, the corruption of his authority; bitterness may be in word or spirit, even without open anger (Wesley). Clarke’s stark warning: where love is wanting in marriage, there is hell upon earth. The balance: submission without love is tyranny, love without order is weakness, but the two together make the Christian home.
20:
On Colossians 3:20 — “Children, obey your parents in all things: for this is well pleasing unto the Lord” — the witnesses mark the children’s duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the scope. Robertson: “obey” (hypakouete, hearken under); “in all things” the hard part, continual not occasional. Adam Clarke: “in everything… not contrary to the will or word of God.” The Reformed Calvin notes the limit if parents command the unlawful. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: honor the fathers of the flesh as the Law enjoins.

The verse gives the children’s duty. Children are to obey their parents in all things, for this pleases the Lord. Obedience is the child’s honor.

So the witnesses see Paul lay the duty of obedience upon children. The witnesses note the command: “children, obey your parents” — the listening, heeding obedience that hearkens to and submits to the parents’ authority. They mark the demanding scope: “in all things” — not occasional or selective obedience, but full and continual; this, Robertson notes, is the hard part for the child. They observe the universal qualification the expositors rightly add: obedience “in all things” is bounded, as Clarke and Calvin note, by the will of God — the child is to obey in everything not contrary to God’s word; no parent’s command can require sin. They note the motive: “for this is well pleasing unto the Lord” — childhood obedience is acceptable and pleasing to God, a thing he approves and rewards, lifting the duty above mere family order to a service rendered to the Lord. They observe Cyril’s point that honoring earthly fathers is enjoined by the Law and the Prophets. The verse thus gives the third household duty: the full, willing obedience of children to parents, pleasing to the Lord — matched, as the marriage pair was, by the corresponding duty of fathers not to provoke, which the next verse enjoins.

So the witnesses see Paul lay the duty of obedience upon children. The command: “children, obey your parents” — the heeding obedience that submits to parental authority. The demanding scope: “in all things” — not selective but full and continual, the hard part for the child. The universal qualification the expositors add: obedience is bounded by God’s will — in everything not contrary to his word; no command can require sin. The motive: “well pleasing unto the Lord” — childhood obedience is acceptable to God, lifting the duty above mere family order to a service rendered to the Lord himself.
21:
On Colossians 3:21 — “Fathers, provoke not your children to anger, lest they be discouraged” — the witnesses mark the fathers’ duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the warning. Robertson: “provoke not” (me erethizete), do not nag as a habit; “that they be not discouraged” (athymosin). Barnes: lest by continual fault-finding “they lose all courage.” Ellicott: the true reading is “provoke not to emulation… restless stimulation.” Wesley: lest they turn “desperate or stupid.”

The verse gives the matching duty for fathers. Fathers are not to provoke their children, lest they be discouraged. Authority must not crush the child’s spirit.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the children’s obedience with a charge to fathers. The witnesses note the warning: “provoke not your children to anger” — do not exasperate, irritate, or nag them, whether by harshness, continual fault-finding, unreasonable demands, or, as Ellicott reads the better text, restless over-stimulation, the constant goading even of the willing child. They mark the reason: “lest they be discouraged” — lest, by being perpetually found fault with and never able to please, the children lose heart, give up trying, and sink into despair or sullen stupidity. They observe the wisdom and tenderness of the apostle’s practical insight: harsh, nagging severity does not produce obedience but breaks the child’s spirit and provokes resentment or despair. They note Wesley’s point that such provocation may turn the child desperate or stupid — either rebellious or crushed. They observe the balance of the household code once more: the child’s duty of obedience is matched by the father’s duty not to embitter; authority is to be exercised so as to encourage, not to discourage. The verse thus completes the parent-child pair: fathers are to wield their authority gently, never provoking or crushing, that the children may obey from love and not lose heart — before Paul turns to the longest of the household pairs, servants and masters.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the children’s obedience with a charge to fathers. The warning: “provoke not your children to anger” — do not exasperate or nag them, whether by harshness, continual fault-finding, or, as Ellicott reads the better text, restless over-stimulation, the constant goading even of the willing child. The reason: “lest they be discouraged” — lest, never able to please, they lose heart and sink into despair or sullen stupidity (Wesley: desperate or stupid). The balance of the code: the child’s obedience matched by the father’s duty not to embitter; authority is to encourage, not crush.
22:
On Colossians 3:22 — “Servants, obey in all things your masters according to the flesh; not with eyeservice, as menpleasers; but in singleness of heart, fearing God” — the witnesses mark the servants’ duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the inward spirit. Robertson: “masters according to the flesh” (earthly lords, though Christ is Lord); “not with eyeservice” (ophthalmodouliais, a Pauline word). Wesley: “eyeservice — more diligent under their eye… singleness of heart… fearing God.” The Reformed Calvin: service to men is done so that Christ is served.

The verse gives the servants’ duty. Servants are to obey their earthly masters in all things, not with mere outward show before the eye, but sincerely, fearing God. Service is to be hearty and God-fearing.

So the witnesses see Paul instruct servants — in that day, slaves — in the spirit of true Christian service. The witnesses note the command: “obey in all things your masters according to the flesh” — full obedience to their earthly lords (“according to the flesh,” for these had a higher Lord in Christ) in all lawful things. They mark the negative: “not with eyeservice, as menpleasers” — not the false diligence that works only while watched, putting on a show to please the master’s eye while idle behind his back. They observe the positive spirit: “in singleness of heart” — with a sincere, single-minded honesty of purpose, doing the work truly and not for show; and “fearing God” — acting from reverence for God, who sees all, rather than mere dread of the human master. They note that this transforms even the lowliest service: the Christian slave serves not merely men but God, and his honest, wholehearted work is a religious duty rendered in God’s sight. They observe how the gospel dignifies and purifies the servant’s lot, making sincere, God-fearing diligence the rule. The verse thus opens the servant-master pair: the servant is to obey sincerely and heartily, fearing God, not playing the eye-pleaser — a service that, as the next verses show, is done to the Lord and rewarded by him.

So the witnesses see Paul instruct servants — in that day, slaves — in the spirit of true Christian service. The command: “obey in all things your masters according to the flesh” — full obedience to their earthly lords (for these had a higher Lord in Christ) in all lawful things. The negative: “not with eyeservice, as menpleasers” — not the false diligence that works only while watched. The positive spirit: “in singleness of heart” (sincere, single-minded honesty) and “fearing God” (from reverence for the God who sees all). The gospel dignifies the servant’s lot: he serves not merely men but God.
23:
On Colossians 3:23 — “And whatsoever ye do, do it heartily, as to the Lord, and not unto men” — the witnesses mark the heart of true service.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the spirit. Robertson: “heartily” (ek psyches), from the soul, not mere eye-service; “as unto the Lord” even when serving men (Ephesians 6:7). Wesley: “cheerfully, diligently… the single-hearted are never displeased… because they have another aim.”

The verse gives the principle of all service. Whatever you do, do it heartily, as to the Lord and not to men. Work done for Christ is done from the soul.

So the witnesses see Paul lift the servant’s work, and all work, to a service of Christ done from the heart. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “whatsoever ye do” — every task, however lowly, falls under this rule, echoing the great maxim of verse 17. They mark the manner: “do it heartily” — from the soul, with the whole heart, cheerfully and diligently, not grudgingly or with mere outward show. They observe the transforming aim: “as to the Lord, and not unto men” — the work, though done for an earthly master, is to be rendered as service to Christ himself, with the eye on him rather than on men. They note Wesley’s beautiful point that the single-hearted, who serve thus, are never dejected or disappointed by the treatment of those they serve, because their true aim is higher — they look to the Lord, whom good or evil treatment from men cannot disappoint. They observe that this principle ennobles all honest labor: the humblest task done for Christ becomes worship and is freed from the bitterness of grudging or man-pleasing service. The verse thus gives the inward secret of the servant’s duty, and indeed of all Christian work: to do everything heartily, as unto the Lord — a service to Christ that, as the next verse promises, he himself will reward.

So the witnesses see Paul lift the servant’s work, and all work, to a service of Christ done from the heart. The comprehensiveness: “whatsoever ye do” — every task, however lowly, echoing verse 17. The manner: “do it heartily” — from the soul, cheerfully and diligently, not with mere outward show. The transforming aim: “as to the Lord, and not unto men” — the work, though done for an earthly master, rendered as service to Christ himself. Wesley’s point: the single-hearted are never dejected by the treatment of those they serve, because their aim is higher. The humblest task done for Christ becomes worship.
24:
On Colossians 3:24 — “Knowing that of the Lord ye shall receive the reward of the inheritance: for ye serve the Lord Christ” — the witnesses mark the servant’s reward.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the recompense. Robertson: “ye shall receive” (apolempsesthe, recover, get back) “the recompense” (antapodosin); given back in return. Adam Clarke: “here ye have neither lands nor property… ye have an inheritance in store.” Gill: though they receive little from earthly masters, they are treated as children by the Lord.

The verse gives the servant’s encouragement. Knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward; for you serve the Lord Christ. The lowliest servant has a glorious recompense.

So the witnesses see Paul encourage the Christian servant with the promise of a heavenly reward. The witnesses note the certainty: “knowing that of the Lord ye shall receive the reward” — the servant who works heartily as to the Lord may be sure of a recompense from him. They mark the wonder of it: “the reward of the inheritance” — not the wages of a slave, but an inheritance, the portion of a son; though the earthly servant owns nothing and may receive little or nothing from his master, he has stored up for him a heavenly inheritance. They observe Clarke’s comfort: here the slave has neither lands nor property, but let him be faithful, and Christ will give him a heavenly inheritance. They note the ground: “for ye serve the Lord Christ” — the Christian servant’s true Master is Christ; his honest work, done for his earthly lord, is service to Christ, who will reward it as such. They observe how this transforms the slave’s hard lot: he serves not a mere man but the Lord, and his recompense is not earthly wages but an eternal inheritance. The verse thus crowns the servant’s duty with the highest motive and comfort: faithful service, done to the Lord Christ, will be repaid with the inheritance of glory — even where earthly masters give no reward, as the closing verse’s warning of impartial justice confirms.
25:
On Colossians 3:25 — “But he that doeth wrong shall receive for the wrong which he hath done: and there is no respect of persons” — the witnesses mark the impartial justice of God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the scope. Robertson: “shall receive again” (komisetai) the wrong done; unclear whether the wrongdoer is master or slave — Lightfoot says both. Ellicott: “clearly here the master” (Ephesians 6:9), though not limited to him. Adam Clarke: God sees the unfaithful and “will punish.”

The verse closes with a warning of impartial judgment. The wrongdoer will be repaid for his wrong, and there is no partiality with God. Justice falls alike on all.

So the witnesses see Paul close the household code with the solemn assurance of God’s impartial justice. The witnesses note the warning: “he that doeth wrong shall receive for the wrong” — the doer of injustice will be repaid in kind, recompensed by God for the wrong he has committed. They mark the debated reference: it is unclear whether the wrongdoer is the master or the slave; Ellicott takes it chiefly of the master (the parallel in Ephesians warns masters), while Lightfoot, the witnesses observe, applies it to both — the principle holds either way. They observe the great equalizer: “there is no respect of persons” — God shows no partiality; he does not favor the master over the slave or the great over the lowly, but judges all by the same standard. They note the comfort and the warning combined: the wronged servant may be sure God will right his cause, and the wronging master may be sure he will not escape because of his rank. They observe that this impartial justice undergirds the whole household ethic: every duty is rendered, ultimately, before a God who repays each according to his deeds, regardless of station. The verse thus seals the section: God’s even-handed justice, which no earthly inequality can sway, stands over master and servant alike — a sober close to the household code and to the practical heart of the chapter, urging all to do right in the sight of an impartial Judge.
